# Questions in Livestream 127

Live Stream #127: Pyramid of the Puppet Pharaohs

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=354" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:54</a> | Is there an Egyptian version of Lilith? | Sort of, but he explains Lilith mainly as a later Second Temple and Talmudic figure rather than a direct Egyptian equivalent. | He says the short answer is no if the question means a direct Egyptian equivalent to Lilith as a demonic mother figure. He explains Lilith first as a night-demon tradition that develops in Second Temple and later Talmudic material, with the Adam's-first-wife form being much later. Egyptian religion, in his framing, does not have a pure evil being of that sort because Egyptian deities normally have both beneficial and dangerous aspects. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=646" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:46</a> | Did Moses have horns, or does Exodus 34:33-35 mean his face shone? | He reads the passage as Moses' face shining, not as Moses literally having horns. | He explains that the Hebrew word can belong to a lexical range involving shining or horns, and that Jerome's horn translation helped the horned-Moses tradition gain traction in Western Christianity. He allows that a symbolic horn reading has some plausibility in light of royal iconography where kings display animal features as signs of power. What he rejects is the literal idea that Moses physically grew horns. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=973" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:13</a> | Does the etymology of names in Genesis tell us anything about the time or people Genesis refers to? | Not much. He says many of the names are too vague or uncertain to carry that kind of historical weight. | He says the names in Genesis usually do not provide as much historical information as people want because many meanings are uncertain and proposed connections are not solid enough. Using Seth as the example, he says the Hebrew name and the Egyptian god Seth only look alike in English; the Hebrew and Egyptian forms are different and are not etymologically related. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=1178" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:38</a> | Would the biblical authors have embraced the idea of God's absolute immutability as defended by Aristotle and Aquinas? | He says yes, and points to biblical language about God's unchangeability. | He points to texts such as Malachi 3:6 and language about Christ being the same yesterday, today, and forever as evidence that biblical writers already think of God as unchanging. He argues immutability is essential because God's promises would not be dependable if God could change in his being or commitments. He distinguishes this from human emotional change by saying God's omniscience means he does not move through changing emotional states as humans do. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=1492" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:52</a> | Do Talmudic claims about Bezalel's miraculous craftsmanship fit the Exodus text? | He reads the text itself as emphasizing Spirit-given wisdom and skill without needing every later embellishment. | He says Exodus states that Bezalel was filled with the Spirit, wisdom, understanding, knowledge, and craftsmanship, but it does not specify that the skills appeared without prior training. He treats the later claim that Bezalel could not have learned from Egyptians as eisegesis, arguing that divine inspiration can include a person's whole life, apprenticeship, and work experience. On that model, God prepared Bezalel through Egyptian workshop skills and then used him as an inspired craftsman. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=2143" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:43</a> | What are his thoughts on the work of John Hilber and Victor Matthews? | He cannot say much about Matthews, but he generally likes Hilber's Old Testament work when Hilber stays in that lane. | He says he has not read enough Victor Matthews to comment responsibly. For John Hilber, he likes the Old Testament work but is more cautious when Hilber moves into Egyptian material, because Egyptology is language-driven and primary-source heavy. He says scholars who rely on translations can make mistakes in Egyptian contexts even when their biblical scholarship is otherwise valuable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=2500" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:40</a> | Why are Christians not observing Torah now when the commands say "forever" in many places? | He explains the issue through fulfillment in Christ, covenant change, and the distinction between moral and ritual law. | He says Torah has to be read contextually because it can refer to the Pentateuch, ritual law, or other legal material. He argues Jeremiah's new-covenant language and Christ's fulfillment of the law mean the sacrificial system has been superseded: sacrifices covered sin like whiteout, but Christ takes sin away. Christians therefore do not practice the ritual sacrificial law, while moral commands such as prohibitions on theft and adultery still stand. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=4462" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:22</a> | Can Shasu and Apiru be considered synonyms? | No. He distinguishes Shasu as wandering tribal groups and Apiru as a different social category. | He says Shasu are an ethnonym for wandering groups associated with Edom, Midian, and the Sinai, while Apiru is a social class term for outsiders, mercenaries, soldiers, or people living beyond respectable urban society. He also distinguishes their distribution and chronology: Apiru appear across a much wider region and over a longer period, while Shasu are tied especially to the southern Levant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=4727" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:47</a> | Did ancient Egypt have smoke-fumigation rituals for healing and cleanliness? | Yes. He identifies the practice as burning incense or fragrant wood for purifying and ritual purposes. | He describes a common practice of burning incense or fragrant wood to create smoke for purification. Examples include passing hands through smoke, cleansing a shrine before installing a deity, and waving incense with a spoon before royal or ritual objects. He mentions myrrh, frankincense, sandalwood, relief evidence, textual evidence, and even an Elephantine Aramaic text involving Israelites performing such a rite for a goddess. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=4924" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:04</a> | What is his favorite research library that he has visited? | He names the University of Liverpool's library because of the resources he used there. | He chooses the University of Liverpool's library because, for his work, it had the best combination of collection quality and easy access to resources. He contrasts that with libraries that may have strong collections or beautiful buildings but are inefficient for quickly finding and using books. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=5131" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:31</a> | Does he think John son of Zebedee wrote the Gospel of John, or someone else? | He says he is not certain and keeps the answer cautious. | He says he does not have a firm view because he has not studied the authorship question enough to decide between options. He notes that the Gospel is identified with John and was recognized early, so he is not treating it as a forgery, but John was a common name. John son of Zebedee and John the Elder are both possible in his answer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=5248" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:28</a> | How did Egyptians practice prayer, and did they have anything like mystical or ecstatic prayer? | He says Egyptians did practice prayer, and then compares Egyptian and Israelite prayer with caveats. | He says ordinary Egyptians often prayed outside temples because only priests and certain scribes could enter sacred spaces. They used carved ears and ear amulets on temple walls as symbols that the gods could hear prayers. He is unsure about mystical or ecstatic prayer, but notes similarities with Israelite practice such as praying prostrate and praying from outer courts or outside the most sacred area. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=5491" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:31</a> | What is the history of the Exodus mythicist position, and who knows more Latin? | He says Exodus mythicism grows out of critical and minimalist scholarship, and jokes that Dark Blade probably knows more Latin. | He jokes that Dark Blade probably knows more Latin because he never had the chance to take Latin despite wanting to. On Exodus mythicism, he says skepticism has roots in nineteenth-century critical scholarship and the documentary hypothesis, then gains more explicit mythicist form through biblical minimalism in the 1970s and 1980s. In his summary, the modern version is recent even though its roots go back further. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=5668" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:28</a> | Do Canadians learn other languages besides French? | French is required in school, though how much one learns depends on the student and program. | He says Canadian students get French in school, with the amount depending on the student and province or program. In his experience, students had to take an approved language, theoretically French, German, or Spanish, but most schools offered only French, making French effectively mandatory. He adds that students could sometimes transfer later, but French instruction typically began earlier. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=8286" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:06</a> | If he could hang out with John Wesley, John Calvin, or Charles Spurgeon, who would it be? | He chooses from the options conversationally, treating it as a light historical-personality question. | He chooses Spurgeon, saying Calvin would offer interesting theological conversation but Spurgeon seems more interesting as a person. He praises Spurgeon's sermons as insightful and expressive, adds a story about pastors plagiarizing Spurgeon, and says Spurgeon would be the most interesting of the three to spend time with. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=8729" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:29</a> | What are his thoughts on the Rashi and Walton views of Genesis 3:15? | He says he usually agrees with Walton but would need to read the specific claim; he treats Genesis 3:15 as having competing interpretive traditions. | He says he does not know Walton's specific view well enough to give a firm answer, though he often agrees with Walton on Genesis. If the point concerns snake ethology and the relationship between snakes and people, he would agree to some extent. His main qualification is that the snake is a symbol and not the ultimate creature or being behind the event. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=9324" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:24</a> | What are his thoughts on alleged incidents of some Jews in the Holy Land spitting on Christian visitors? | He says people should not treat each other that way and frames it as bad behavior rather than something to excuse. | He calls the behavior disgraceful and says Torah itself commands love of neighbor and warns against persecuting foreigners. He broadens the point to include mistreatment of Christians and Messianic Jews, saying some incidents are worse than spitting, including stone throwing. His answer frames such actions as contrary to Israel's calling to be a priestly witness to the world. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=9663" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:03</a> | Were badly handicapped Egyptian babies thrown into the Nile? | No. He says Egyptians generally abhorred infanticide. | He says Egyptians generally abhorred infanticide and even viewed child sacrifice among Levantine peoples as savage. That is why Pharaoh's killing of Israelite boys would have been morally shocking within normal Egyptian values. He adds that some depictions show disabled Egyptians living productive lives, though survival with severe disabilities was still harder in a Late Bronze Age context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=9754" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:34</a> | Which pharaoh does he think was involved in the killing of Israelite boys? | He says it was probably King Aye, while acknowledging the reconstruction is contextual. | He says it was probably King Aye, describing him as paranoid and connected with conflict involving the Hittites. The answer is presented as a likelihood rather than a demonstrated certainty, and it appears in the context of explaining why the order to kill Israelite boys would have been abnormal and morally abhorrent in Egyptian society. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=9861" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:21</a> | Is it true that some Jews at Elephantine worshiped Anat? | Yes. He says they worshiped Anat and other deities they should not have worshiped. | He says yes: the Elephantine Jews worshiped Anat and other deities, offered wine and incense, and fell into local Egyptian religious patterns. He links this to Jeremiah's warning about returning to Egypt, saying they began with a Yahweh temple but soon adopted local divine worship they should not have practiced. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=10317" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:51:57</a> | Would it be legal to offer animals publicly for a rebuilt temple? | He says animal-rights activists would object, but frames the issue as one of public sanction and ritual context. | He says activists would strongly object, but if a temple were rebuilt the publicly sanctioned ritual sacrificial system would come back into view. He stresses that the commandments governing temple worship would become operative again, creating practical, legal, public-order, and security problems around sacrifices, pilgrimage, and the visible realities of animal offerings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=10494" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:54</a> | Why does he think some will try to rebuild the temple? | He connects the expectation to New Testament language about the abomination of desolation in a holy place. | He reasons from Matthew 24:15 and Daniel's abomination of desolation, arguing that an Israelite holy place presupposes a temple context. He says previous events can function as types, but he expects an ultimate fulfillment requiring a holy place on the Temple Mount. He also mentions Ezekiel's temple measurements as another reason he thinks a future temple is likely. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=10776" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:36</a> | Do we know what hieroglyphs Joseph's Egyptian name would have used? | He says we have a good idea and begins working from the Egyptian form of the name. | He works from Genesis 41:45 and says the Egyptian form behind Joseph's name would mean something like "he who is given as the living one." He says the hieroglyphic form can be reconstructed with a good degree of confidence, but the chance of finding Joseph's actual name in an inscription is very small because documentary preservation from that region and period is poor. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=10955" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:02:35</a> | If Egyptians found infanticide abominable, did that include abortion and abortion facilities? | Yes for abortion as abominable; he says Egypt did not have abortion facilities like those known elsewhere. | He says the Egyptian rejection of infanticide would include abortion, and he denies that Egypt had abortion facilities. He contrasts Egypt with places where he says such practices appear, including Mesopotamia, Rome, and Phoenician settlements such as Carthage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=11000" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:20</a> | What happened if an arranged marriage in Egypt failed because one person disliked the other? | He says divorce was available and nobody was compelled to remain in the marriage. | He says divorce in Egypt was easy and did not usually require going through courts. No one, male or female, was compelled to stay married, and property was generally divided on a one-third and two-thirds basis because the house counted as a major part of wealth. He adds that divorce carried little stigma and repeated marriages were not unusual, though children made marriages more stable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=11860" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:40</a> | Why did Egypt not send wives to foreign courts? | He says Egypt viewed the other countries as beneath them and had neither the need nor desire to send royal women. | He says Egypt received treaty wives but almost never sent Egyptian women out because Egyptians were ethnocentric, thought other nations were beneath them, and did not need to make that concession. The rare case he mentions is a daughter sent to Solomon during a dynasty in severe decline. In general, accepting foreign wives increased Egyptian prestige, while sending Egyptian women abroad did not. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=12043" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:43</a> | How were the descendants of foreign wives seen if they were not murdered, and how did they fit into society? | They were worked into the bureaucracy, army, or court service rather than put in line for the throne. | He says a large bureaucracy absorbed these children: they became overseers, crown ministers, soldiers, generals, and other trusted servants of the state. They were usually not in line for the throne but could be useful and trusted in administration. He compares this to Israelite practice and gives Ramesses II's children as examples of royal offspring being assigned diplomatic, military, or restoration roles. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=12196" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:16</a> | Did Israelite kings copy Egyptians in taking multiple wives? | He says they copied a broad ancient Near Eastern royal custom, not only Egypt. | He says Israelite kings copied a widespread ancient Near Eastern royal practice rather than simply imitating Egypt. Everyone in that royal world did this, including Egyptians and other Levantine rulers. His answer treats multiple royal wives as a broader political and cultural custom of kingship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=12290" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:50</a> | What about royal multiple-wife practices in the ancient Far East? | He says Chinese emperors had harems, while he is less certain about Korea and Japan. | He says Chinese emperors are known to have had harems, so the practice existed there. He is not sure about Koreans, and he says Japanese emperors do not seem to have done it, though he is unsure about the earliest Japanese emperors. The answer is intentionally cautious outside the Chinese example. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=12440" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:27:20</a> | Was removing shoes before a god an Egyptian practice, and is it evidence for the Torah? | No. He says Egyptians replaced shoes after ritual bathing rather than removing them before a god. | He says Egyptian priests did not simply remove shoes before the gods or go barefoot in the temple. After ritual bathing, they removed animal products such as leather sandals and wore papyrus sandals inside the temple. Because the practice is replacement after purification rather than barefoot removal before a deity, he does not treat it as evidence for the Torah scene. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hqbQIlTCGTE?t=12548" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:08</a> | Is Hur the father of Uri the same Hur who helped hold Moses' arms up? | Yes. He identifies Hur as the same person and notes the Egyptian connection of the name. | He says it is the same Hur, and identifies the name as essentially Horus, an Egyptian name. He notes that this matters because Hur is from Judah, so it is an example of a Judahite with an Egyptian name rather than Egyptian names being limited to Levites. He also describes Hur as highly important in Israel after Aaron. |
